summary: Fowler argues that the common metaphor of 'software architect' fundamentally misunderstands the actual role of building architects. Building architects primarily handle client interaction and focus on layout and appearance, but are not responsible for structural integrity—that's the structural engineer's job. The metaphor suggests software architects are chief designers, but building architects are actually one professional among several who must collaborate effectively. Fowler warns that putting any single role in charge of the customer relationship requires effective cross-discipline communication, which often fails in practice.
key_points: Building architects focus on client interaction and aesthetics, not structural integrity—that responsibility belongs to structural engineers
The closest software equivalent to a building architect is actually a user-interface designer, not a technical architect
Architects who control the client relationship often resist changes from other disciplines even when those changes would significantly reduce costs
User-interface designers often fail to account for deeper technological concerns like concurrency and locking strategies
Building architects, despite winning the battle to control building design, are typically the lowest paid skilled workers on the job
money_quotes: A software architect is seen as a chief designer, someone who pulls together everything on the project. But this is not what a building architect does.
An architect is not responsible for the structural integrity of the building. That's the role of the structural engineer.
Given the focus on client interaction I think the closest equivalent in software development is a user-interface designer.
One of the problems I've seen with the world of otherwise excellent user-interface people is that they don't take into account the costs of different user-interface schemes.
Dave Rice likes to point out that an application's concurrency and locking strategies cannot be separated from the user-interface experience.
Software Architects: be careful what you wish for!
key_insight: The building architect metaphor for software architects is doubly misleading: it both overstates the technical authority of building architects and understates the need for cross-discipline collaboration in any complex design work.
